title: UBI8-INIT: Systemd unit capture logging
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

A continuation off https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/16923.
On my host, I am running this container as a Systemd service and I am unable to capture log output to Journald.

Here is my unit file.
```
## ANSIBLE MANAGED
[Unit]
Description=Podman papercut.service
Documentation=man:podman-run(1)
Documentation=https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/8/html/building_running_and_managing_containers/assembly_porting-containers-to-systemd-using-podman_building-running-and-managing-containers
Wants=network-online.target
BindsTo=
After=network-online.target
After=
PartOf=
RequiresMountsFor=%t/containers

[Service]
Environment=PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=%n
ExecStartPre=-/usr/bin/podman rm -f --ignore --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id
ExecStartPre=-/bin/rm -f %t/%n.ctr-id
ExecStartPre=-/bin/rm -f %t/%n.pid
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c ' \
    /usr/bin/podman run \
    --name papercut \
    -d \
    --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id --sdnotify=conmon --cgroups=no-conmon \
    --conmon-pidfile=%t/%n.pid \
    --restart no \
    --replace \
    --rm \
    --stop-timeout 60 \
    --log-driver=passthrough \
    -v /etc/localtime:/etc/localtime:ro \
    -v %E/papercut/lpadmin.env:/lpadmin.env:ro \
    -v %E/papercut/spool/:/var/cups/spool/:rw \
    -v %E/papercut/config/:/home/papercut/pc-mobility-print/data/config:rw \
    -l=diun.enable \
    -t \
    --env-file %E/%N/%N.env \
    --systemd=always \
    docker.io/notmycloud/papercut-mp:latest \
    '
ExecReload=-/usr/bin/podman stop --ignore --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id
ExecReload=-/usr/bin/podman rm -f --ignore --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id
ExecStop=/usr/bin/podman stop --ignore --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id
ExecStopPost=/usr/bin/podman rm -f --ignore --cidfile=%t/%n.ctr-id
Type=notify
NotifyAccess=all
KillMode=mixed
PIDFile=%t/%n.pid
Restart=on-failure
SyslogIdentifier=%N

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
WantedBy=default.target
```

I have tried without the `--log-driver` flag as well as the `journald` and `none` options.
I have also tried with and without the `-t`, but I have found that when running manually in my shell I need that option to get output when Systemd in the container takes over.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.3.1
API Version:  4.3.1
Go Version:   go1.19.4
Built:        Wed Dec 31 18:00:00 1969
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman` or `brew info podman`):**

NOTE: I am using the Alvistack repo to get the latest Podman on Debian.
```
podman/unknown,now 100:4.3.1-1 amd64 [installed]
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked [the Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)?**


Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
Physical Debian GNU/Linux 11 (bullseye)

comments.body: How are you trying to capture the logs? The logs should be visible via `journalctl`.

Yes, I am attempting to use `journalctl` on the host, but all I get is the Systemd logs.
```
Jan 01 18:32:00 chuwi systemd[1]: Starting Podman papercut.service...
Jan 01 18:32:00 chuwi systemd[1]: Started Podman papercut.service.
Jan 01 18:32:00 chuwi papercut[2093635]: 4267916ee429f03ab2cf1952080f8f1d01aa5b498f886947c4630056b0ff1980
```
If I use `journalctl` inside the container, then I can see the logs appropriately, but that is a bit of a pain to have to run `podman exec -it papercut journalctl` just to see the logs.

I can see the logs of the container in the journal (see below):
```
~ $ podman run --log-driver=journald --name=issue16967 --rm alpine echo "this is container issue16967"
this is container issue16967
~ $ journalctl --user -r |grep "this is container issue16967"
Jan 03 10:16:26 nebuchadnezzar issue16967[171287]: this is container issue16967
```

You can also use `journalctl --user CONTAINER_NAME=$NAME`.  Does that work on your end?

This looks like a systemd inside of the container, which is taking all of the logs and putting them in a separate location.

The podman logs command is only going to show you the output of the systemd process not the processes inside of the container which are writing to journald.

> @vrothberg I apologize, I forgot to include the following when setting to `journald` mode. `Error: failed to initialize journal: journald logging only enabled with systemd on linux: no support for this OS yet` This is on Debian and I am running rootfull for this container.

That looks like a Debian-specific problem.  It should just work.

> @rhatdan When I run the container just as a `podman run` command in my terminal, as long as I use `-t` I see the entrypoint output and systemd output from the container. However, when I put that command in the unit file, I get no output, either in `podman logs` or on the host journal. I have other containers that I run in a similar way with similar settings that do log to the journal.

In that case, it's likely that the systemd inside the container is eating the logs.

Systemd inside the container is in fact recording the logs internally, however, I do have journald inside the container set to forward to the wall, which, to my understanding should send the logs additionally to the tty provided with `-t`.

Podman is going to record stdout and stderr from pid1.  I am not sure hat the "wall" is?  Does this mean that systemd process running as pid 1 inside of the container will start writing everything in the journal to stdout/stderr?

I misspoke, Journald is configured to write to the console as per the documentation [here](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/journald.conf.html#:~:text=MaxLevelStore%3D%2C,journald.max_level_wall%3D%22.).

As a test, I attempted to write to both `/dev/stdout` and `/dev/tty` and neither show up in the host journal.
If you run with a tty attached to the container in foreground, do you see the content on the tty?

If I trim the appropriate parts from my unit file command and run the following:
```bash
/bin/bash -c ' \
    /usr/bin/podman run \
    -d \
    --name test \
    --restart no \
    --replace \
    --rm \
    --stop-timeout 60 \
    -t \
    --systemd=always \
    docker.io/notmycloud/papercut-mp:latest \
    '
```
I am able to get output from `poodman logs test`.
If I run that same command without the `-d`, I get the output on my terminal.
Ok so is there a problem?

It seems to be when utilizing Systemd to run the container that the logs do not get captured anywhere other than the journal instance inside of the container.
I am looking to capture them into the host's journal.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
This seems to be a systemd thing and not something Podman can easily fix.  One other thing you might want to look at is the passthrough log type.
